posts.caption:
@chadmooreholla_ is best known for his tender, spontaneous pictures of New York‚Äôs bright young things, but the American photographer‚Äôs latest photo book is a departure from his usual portraits. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
As the title suggests, Eyes and Skies (published by @superlabo), moves between the micro and the macro as it presents his photographs of sunsets and sunrises, skylines and starry nights alongside his close-up studies of eyes. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Moore first turned his lens skyward during Covid, creating his previous book Anybody Anyway ‚Äì a monograph combining his dreamy portraits with pictures of the cosmos. In a sense, Eyes and Skies picks up where Anybody Anyway left off ‚Äì contemplating the mysteries of the universe. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
‚ÄúI started doing the sky images quite slowly, as I matured with making photos and got bored with waiting around for something to happen. Most of my images rely on someone else ‚Äì typically my friends,‚Äù he tells @ohnoitsemilyd. ‚ÄúI just had the thought, ‚ÄòWhat has the same energy as an emotional portrait of a human subject?‚Äô, and I figured the closest thing would be the stars.‚Äù Read the interview at the link in bio üì≤‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
üì∏ Eyes and Skies by @chadmooreholla_‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
#ChadMoore‚ÅÝ

posts.caption:
John Waters, the self-styled ‚ÄúPope of Trash‚Äù, turns 80 today. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
He‚Äôs the man who spent his teenage years taking LSD in the suburbs of Baltimore with Divine, who he later directed to eat dog shit in the cult film Pink Flamingoes. He‚Äôs also the author of the single best piece of romantic advice ever given: ‚ÄúIf you go home with someone and they don‚Äôt have any books, don‚Äôt fuck them.‚Äù So with the latter quip in mind, we looked back on his interview with #CathClarke featured in the S/S14 issue of AnOther Magazine.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
From dealing with bullies to how to stay young, he offers an unconventional guide to life. Read it at the link in bio üì≤‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
üì∏ #JohnWaters. Photography by @mauricehaasphoto
posts.alt_text: Photo by AnOther Magazine on April 22, 2026. May be an image of standing, magazine and text that says 'AnOther John Waters Waters on Staying Young, Regrets and More'.

posts.caption:
Romanticising our past is part of how families tell stories, but photographer and filmmaker @tirtharabinlawati set that aside when he returned to Nepal with stylist @samtthapa, focusing instead on the country‚Äôs changing youth culture.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
In 2025, the pair travelled back to the country of their births to create Where We Meet, a project spanning two series shown at Solar Beam Kingdom, curated by Skylar Kang and Jeremy Chen: Girls Skate Nepal, on young girls reshaping skate culture in Kathmandu, and Jamkabhet, tracing underground music scenes and youth communities. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
‚ÄúAs someone of Nepalese descent but having grown up predominantly in the UK, I‚Äôve always wanted to understand and connect with what the youth were up to over there,‚Äù says Thapa. @ohnoitsemilyd speaks with the duo about the project at the link in bio üì≤‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
üì∏ Where We Meet. Photography by @tirtharabinlawati. Styling by @samtthapa

posts.caption:
In 1977, in Minneapolis, a 19-year-old musician named Prince Rogers Nelson caught the attention of music agent Owen Husney and his partner Gary Levinson. Keen to have him photographed, they approached @robertwhitmanphoto, playing an early demo of Soft and Wet to draw him in. It worked. Whitman was transfixed, and soon after photographed Nelson, who would become the artist known as Prince.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Ten years after his passing, we revisit those images collected in Prince Pre Fame, a book that gathers the full series. As Whitman puts it, it‚Äôs ‚Äúa book that every Prince fan should have on their table, telling the story of two guys from Minneapolis following their passions, trying to pursue their dreams.‚Äù‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
üì∏ Prince Pre Fame. Photography @robertwhitmanphoto

posts.caption:
The couture house of Gabrielle Chanel was founded in 1915 in the French seaside resort town of Biarritz. Breaking away from the constraints and conventions of Paris, she opened her haute couture house there, bringing together a boutique, atelier, salon, and apartment under one roof, presenting a precursor to the Chanel house that would later be established on Rue Cambon. As Biarritz was a favoured destination for Europe‚Äôs high society and royalty at the time, she was able to achieve rapid success.
Today, Matthieu Blazy‚Äôs Chanel returns to Biarritz for a Cruise 26/27 show. Watch the full video featuring the show teaser filmed in Biarritz. Blazy‚Äôs first Chanel Cruise show will be unveiled on 28 April at 5:30 PM (BST).
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.

London, this is your moment.
Chopova Lowena ‚Äî the cult London-based label known for its rebellious mix of traditional craft influences, punk spirit, a playful, almost ‚Äúcute‚Äù energy, and instantly recognisable carabiner skirts ‚Äî is opening up its archive for a rare one-day sample sale.
Expect one-off pieces, past-season gems, and exclusive finds you won‚Äôt see again. It‚Äôs the kind of drop that defines why the brand has built such a devoted following ‚Äî playful, subversive, and impossible to replicate.
Happening tomorrow only, with restocks throughout the day and strictly limited quantities (yes, one carabiner skirt per person).
No tickets, just show up.
And if you needed one more reason ‚Äî this won‚Äôt happen again until 2028.
See you in the queue.

I‚Äôm a major organiser ‚Äì such a Capricorn,‚Äù she laughs. ‚Äú[With] my skates on, the only thing I can think about is what I‚Äôm doing. posts.caption:
For the global luxury industry, Salone del Mobile in Milan has become a moment where brands look beyond the runway to expand into the broader "lifestyle" economy. At the centre of this intersection is @DimoreStudio, co-founded by Britt Moran (@brittmoranmilano) and @EmilianoSalci ‚Äî a studio that has defined the aesthetic language for luxury hospitality, retail and private residential projects worldwide.
As luxury conglomerates increasingly pursue the "home" and hospitality categories to drive long-term growth, Britt Moran offers an insider's perspective on why credibility in this space can't be bought ‚Äî it has to be built.
This week on The BoF Podcast, Moran joins BoF founder @ImranAmed during Milan Design Week to discuss what fashion brands get wrong about design, why physical spaces still win over screens, and how a partnership that began after a romantic relationship ended became one of the most influential studios in the world.
üéôÔ∏è Listen now #linkinbio #TheBoFPodcast
üì∑ Stefano Galuzzi
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on April 25, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, magazine, poster and text that says 'BOF The BoF Podcast Podeast Britt Moran BrittMoranonWhv on Why Atmosphere Is a Real Luxury Product'.

posts.caption:
Hi this is @ImranAmed, welcome to my weekly letter.
Each year during Milan Design Week, I make a point of visiting @laboratorioparavicini, a small ceramics atelier. I‚Äôve admired their work for years, but this time I got to meet the people behind it ‚Äî and it changed how I understood the place entirely.
Costanza Paravicini founded the workshop in the 1990s. More than three decades later, she still runs the company with her daughters Benedetta and Margherita. There is an elegance about these women that is ineffably Italian: You can tell immediately that they are doing this work because they love it.
Sales flow through their own e-commerce site as well as curated platforms. But they can‚Äôt really keep up with demand. So, at times, the family turns to technology to help achieve more scale: A handpainted plate costs around ‚Ç¨300, while a digitally printed version runs closer to ‚Ç¨115.
It is into this more traditional world of craft that technology arrived with considerable ambition at this year‚Äôs Design Week.
@Samsung‚Äôs first-ever chief design officer Mauro Porcini staged his inaugural Salone installation at Superstudio Pi√π: ‚ÄúDesign Is an Act of Love,‚Äù an exhibition spanning 12 immersive zones.
We‚Äôve heard that vision before. Remember Clippy, the animated paperclip that popped up unbidden in Microsoft Office? Or Apple‚Äôs Siri, which has never quite made the interaction feel natural? But this time there was much more attention paid to the design of the devices themselves, with the goal of giving technology more of a human quality. The exhibition opened with a choreographed dance of Samsung devices that reappeared throughout the rest of the show.
Over at @Nike‚Äôs Air_Lab installation, chief design officer Martin Lotti talked me through the brand‚Äôs 50-year history of ‚Äúdesigning with air.‚Äù The brand showcased an inflatable puffer jacket with air bags that can be filled using a small pump, as well as a running shirt engineered with openings that capture air to cool the body ‚Äî demonstrated by a runner on a treadmill, with heat tracking on a screen showing the technology at work.
Read my full letter on where craft, tech and design meet at Design Week #linkinbio

posts.caption:
This week in fashion: Nike faces backlash; Chanel picks Elordi ‚Äî plus other key industry moves.
1. @Moncler reported a 12 percent sales increase in the first quarter, driven by strong demand in Asia, particularly China and Korea.
2. @Gap‚Äôs collaboration with @VictoriaBeckham garnered instant buzz online, with several pieces ‚Äî which run from $34 to $328 ‚Äî selling out within a few hours.
3. Laila Gohar (@lailacooks)‚Äôs reworked antique merry-go-round for @Arket was among the standout activations from fashion brands at the Salone del Mobile design fair Milan, with other notable installations presented by @Gucci and @LoroPiana.
4. Brands that implemented tariff-induced price hikes, including @ElfCosmetics and @Lululemon, are facing class action lawsuits from consumers hoping to recoup their losses after the US government opened a tariff refund portal for businesses this week.
5. Bernard Arnault reaffirmed his confidence in @LVMH at the company‚Äôs annual general meeting this week, even as stagnant sales, a tumbling share price and questions over succession have dented its image.
6. @Chanelofficial named ‚ÄúEuphoria‚Äù star Jacob Elordi as the new face of Bleu de Chanel, replacing Timoth√©e Chalamet, who was named as an ambassador in 2023. Elordi‚Äôs first campaign will drop in May.
7. Advocacy group The Clean Clothes Campaign and four Dutch consumers are suing @Levis, alleging the company misled consumers on the ethics of its labour practices.
8. @Lululemon dismissed activist investor Elliott International‚Äôs wishes to select Ralph Lauren veteran Jane Nielsen as its new CEO, instead picking @Nike executive Heidi O‚ÄôNeill. The news sent its stock price to a 52-week low.
9. @Nike‚Äôs latest attempt to recapture the marketing zeitgeist hit a roadblock after its ad for the Boston Marathon, which read "Runners welcome. Walkers tolerated,‚Äù faced backlash for ‚Äúpace shaming.‚Äù The brand quickly removed the ad.
This Week in Fashion is BoF‚Äôs weekly news briefing, available exclusively to BoF Professional members. Become a BoF Professional member to access the full analysis #linkinbio

posts.caption:
Today, the marketing that happens before a product or brand launch ‚Äî also known as pre-marketing ‚Äî is just as important as what comes after. By stirring anticipation, brands can engineer a splashier launch day.
The challenge for many brands is understanding the tipping point between unveiling too much or too little.
BoF‚Äôs Haley Crawford (@haleycrawf) explores how brands can build anticipation without giving everything away too soon. Read more #linkinbio
üì∑ @isleofmonday, @povbeauty, @realeactives, @setactive
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on April 24, 2026. May be an image of dress, magazine and text that says 'BOF GA Perfecting the Art of Pre-Marketing Brands are turning launches into months-long narratives, building waitlists and dropping hints for consumers to decode. But the strategy only works if they know when to stop teasing and start delivering.'.

posts.caption:
For most of the social-media era, it‚Äôs been an accepted truth that creators with smaller audiences are best at driving sales. But finding those mystical moneymakers was tricky, with an endless number of micro-influencers to choose from and limited data around who actually converts.
That‚Äòs changing fast.
BoF‚Äôs Diana Pearl (@dianaspearl) explores how new measurement tools are redefining influencer value ‚Äî and changing who brands choose to work with. Read more #linkinbio
üì∑ Unsplash

posts.caption:
Luxury entered 2026 with hopes that new creative directors and signs of stabilisation would finally help the sector turn a corner. Instead, the latest round of earnings has raised bigger questions about what growth now looks like for the industry. While brands including Dior, Gucci and Chanel are generating renewed interest, that excitement has not yet translated into a meaningful sales rebound.
From the slowing Chinese market to geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, luxury conglomerates are facing a complex web of challenges that creative hype alone cannot solve.
On the episode, BoF luxury editors Mimosa Spencer (@mimosaspencer) and Robert Williams (@robert.williams.writes) explain why China remains such a critical missing piece, why Louis Vuitton is under closer scrutiny than usual, and why jewellery continues to outperform the rest of luxury.
üéôÔ∏è Listen now #linkinbio #TheDebriefPodcast
üì∑ Unsplash

posts.caption:
Ahan Shetty (@ahan.shetty) moves away from the quintessential superhero mould of Bollywood, emerging instead as one who seems far more relatable. The invincible, larger-than-life Bollywood hero has made room for someone way more emotionally accessible and real. ‚ÄúA decade ago, a leading man was often expected to be larger than life, flawless, and very heroic. Now people just want to see a character who feels real, and has a certain individuality,‚Äù agrees the actor.

posts.caption:
Dear Madam President,
I am writing to urgently request help and protection regarding serious psychological abuse, religious coercion, extortion, harassment, and threats committed against me by an Indian man living in Bangalore, India, named Vikran Vanam, who identifies himself as ‚ÄúGuruji Avadhuta,‚Äù presents himself as a spiritual leader, professor, and is also associated with i Tantra Srividya. His Instagram handle is swaprakashanandaguruji.
He uses his position as a religious leader to manipulate, intimidate, and psychologically abuse people. I have suffered severe emotional distress, fear, and intense mental pressure because of his actions. I believe he has intentionally caused psychological suffering and pushed me toward suicidal thoughts.
I continue to be harassed by this demon, so please, I urgently need help.
I am a woman living in Brazil, and I am asking for urgent international help because I fear for my safety and for the safety of my young daughter. I feel trapped, threatened, and emotionally destroyed by this situation.
There are also reports from other alleged victims describing similar abusive behavior connected to him, including manipulation, coercion, exploitation, and psychological violence. On Reddit, many people are publicly exposing their experiences and reporting similar practices involving this same religious leader, which reinforces the seriousness of the situation and the urgent need for investigation.
I respectfully request that this matter be investigated and that I receive guidance on how I may formally report these abuses and seek protection.
His full name is Vikran Vanam. He is an Indian citizen residing in Bangalore, India, and publicly identifies himself as Senior Guruji Avadhuta through his religious activities, as a professor, and through his presence on social media.
I am fully prepared to provide detailed statements, testimony, and a complete account of how everything happened over the years, including how he operates, how the manipulation began, how the psychological abuse developed, and how he uses his position to control, pressure, and harm victims.
I am willing to provide all available information necessary for the

posts.caption:
Curator Jeffrey Uslip wants everyone to know that everything is normal.
"This is the smoothest exhibition I've curated in 30 years," said Uslip, who has been working on the US Pavilion at the @venice.art.biennale 2026, which opens in two weeks.
Uslip was insistent on the point. "I also just want to be clear, because I know how people talk, it's important to know that we have had complete artistic autonomy throughout this process," he said by phone. Later, by email, he emphasized the process to select the artist ‚Äî the sculptor almaallen ‚Äî was "exactly the same" as it always was.
There's a reason Uslip was adamant. This year's US Pavilion, which is partly funded by the government, has been plagued by controversies and delays. For months, it was uncertain whether the State Department, whose Bureau of Educational and Cultural Affairs oversees the search, would select an artist to present in Venice at all.
Swipe to read more, and see the full story at link in bio.
üì∏: File Photo/Elisabetta Villa/Getty Images | Beowulf Sheehan | Ana Hop | Spencer Wells | Ed Mumford

posts.caption:
Ten years ago today, @beyonce released her pioneering visual album "Lemonade," which solidified the singer and songwriter as one of the most creative and audacious pop stars of her generation. The project also inspired countless trends, including the "Lemonade braids" she wore on the album's cover, her first experimentation with country music in "Daddy Lessons" and a banner season for Red Lobster after she name dropped the chain restaurant in "Formation."
One particular look may define the album: the mustard @roberto_cavalli dress Beyonc√© wears to take a retaliatory baseball bat to parked cars, a fire hydrant, a pi√±ata, and shop windows in her fevered, plucky ode to her lover's betrayal, "Hold Up." The slightly sheer cascade of ruffles, with a thin lace of straps at the neck, hardly grabbed attention when it debuted in the Fall 2016 Roberto Cavalli show, which was inspired by 1970s rock 'n roll glamour.
Stylist @bcompleted plucked it from countless yellow dresses however, and on a grinning, retaliatory Beyonc√©, its louche frills transformed into a statement of feminine power, freedom and vengeance.
üì∏: @beyonce; @parkwood

posts.caption:
"I was there 19 months," @sabato.desarno said of @gucci, "but I've worked in fashion for 23 years."
After a short but widely scrutinized tenure as creative director at the fashion house ‚Äî where his restrained aesthetic struggled to gain commercial traction during a broader downturn in the luxury sector ‚Äî he has been moving across the worlds of design and art with new projects at a more deliberate pace.
Now, during @milan.design.week, he is curating "INSIEME," an exhibition bringing together twelve Italian artisanal companies.
As De Sarno puts it: "I kept thinking about how, when you go to an exhibition or a fair, what you usually see are finished objects. What interested me with 'INSIEME' was turning the attention on everything that comes before that, everything that leads up to the final piece: the mistakes, the decisions, the timing, and those moments when you have to choose a direction and can't go back."
Asked what comes next, De Sarno said: "I see myself simply as Sabato. Someone with different interests, creating conversations with people who are experts in their fields. I'm not an expert in art or design. What I've enjoyed is entering into those conversations and letting projects emerge from them. I don't really feel the need to define it with a title."
üì∏ : Agnese Bedini/DSL Studio

posts.caption:
For teens during the decade, soundtrack albums were must-have merchandise, PARENTAL ADVISORY labels be damned.
The same year as @bazluhrmann's "Romeo + Juliet," "Trainspotting" came out and @iggypopofficial thumping "Lust for Life" set the pace for another CD that was both achingly cool and a massive commercial success. This was the era of "Dangerous Minds" (1995), "Good Will Hunting" (1997), "Cruel Intentions" (1999), and many more great albums that those of a certain age remember as clearly as the movies themselves.
The "Romeo + Juliet" album itself peaked at No.2 on the Billboard 200 chart and went multi-platinum in several countries. Meanwhile, as Marius de Vries, co-music producer on the film points out, films in the '90s were "regularly spawning enormous hit singles." Think @whitneyhouston's "I Will Always Love You" ("The Bodyguard," 1992) or @celinedion's "My Heart Will Go On" (@titanicmovie, 1997).
In the next decade, the rise of online streaming and death of CDs marked the end of the film soundtrack as we knew it. And while the current wave of '90s nostalgia is partly cyclical, it also taps into a longing for a "much more unified mass culture," said @robharvilla, host of the podcast, "60 Songs That Explain the '90s." In the fragmented internet age, "you don't have that same feeling of unity, where everybody is listening to 'Smells Like Teen Spirit' or 'You Outta Know' at the exact same time."
Without a doubt, the '90s were "a bunch of very golden years," de Vries said. "At the time, we were just having a lot of fun, and I think possibly that was reflected in the music we were making."
üì∏ : Merrick Morton/20th Century Fox/Shutterstock/Moviestore Collection Ltd/Alamy Stock Photo/Ronald Grant/Everett Collection

posts.caption:
The editorial moves through a series of characters rather than telling one clear story. Every image feels like a different version of the same idea, shifting between moods, identities, and expressions.
In ‚ÄúUna Canzone‚Äù for Vogue Italia (October 2018), Luigi & Iango take inspiration from Mina Mazzini, one of Italy‚Äôs most iconic singers. She wasn‚Äôt just known for her voice, but for turning her entire image into a form of expression, and that energy runs through the whole story.
The cast, including Guinevere van Seenus, Remington Williams, Karen Elson, and Natasa Vojnovic, constantly transform. One moment the looks are clean and sculptural, the next more exaggerated or playful. The erased brows and strong eye shapes by Erin Parsons reference Mina‚Äôs signature beauty, but feel more modern and experimental here.
Styling by Patti Wilson mixes classic couture with unexpected elements. Ruffles, bold silhouettes, and unusual proportions create a balance between past and future. Hair by Luigi Murenu adds to that transformation, sometimes subtle, sometimes more dramatic, like the bearded look that still feels surprisingly natural.
The more you look at it, the more it starts to make sense. It‚Äôs less about one clear message and more about capturing the mindset of an artist who was always pushing things further.
Credits:
Photography: Luigi & Iango
Styling: Patti Wilson
Makeup: Erin Parsons
Hair: Luigi Murenu
Models: Guinevere van Seenus, Remington Williams, Karen Elson, Natasa Vojnovic
Magazine: Vogue Italia, October 2018

posts.caption:
Madonna‚Äôs style has always leaned toward the bold, but it‚Äôs rarely careless. Her looks are usually clearly thought through, sometimes provocative, sometimes more restraine.
Over time, they‚Äôve become less about fashion alone and more about how she presents herself in a frame.
One of the more notable examples is the cemetery shoot. The contrast is immediate: lingerie layered under a trench coat, heels, sheer tights, and flowers in hand, all set against a graveyard. It‚Äôs an unusual combination, and it creates a strong visual clash between setting and styling. Still, it doesn‚Äôt come across as random. The whole scene feels arranged, like she‚Äôs fully aware of the tension it creates and is working with it rather than against it.
Then there‚Äôs the red background image with the black sequined dress. It‚Äôs much cleaner in comparison. The silhouette is simple, the styling is minimal, with short blonde hair and a steady, direct pose. There‚Äôs no real attempt at excess here. Instead, it feels precise and structured, the kind of image that stands out because of its clarity.
The black leather outfit takes an even more stripped-back approach. Gloves, heels, fishnets, sunglasses, all fairly straightforward pieces, worn with a relaxed confidence. She‚Äôs seated, composed, and the attitude does most of the work. Nothing feels added for effect.
Across these different looks, what remains consistent is the way she presents them. The clothes change, but the sense of control doesn‚Äôt.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINE‚ìá on April 23, 2026. May be an image of costume, dress, poster and text that says 'Madonna' AH Time, Most Iconic Outfits. CULT¬Æ'.

posts.caption:
In Tim Walker‚Äôs ‚ÄúCheck-Mate‚Äù editorial for Vogue Italia (December 2015), everything feels a bit unreal, like stepping into a strange version of a fairytale.
Edie Campbell moves through different sets that look like they‚Äôre taken from a dream. There are chessboards, oversized props, bold colors, and details that feel slightly off, but in a way that still looks visually right. It‚Äôs playful, but not in a childish way, more like a mix between fantasy and high fashion.
The clothes shift between structured couture and more exaggerated, almost costume-like pieces. Some looks are very clean and tailored, others are more dramatic, but they all fit into the same world. Nothing feels random, even when it looks unusual.
Hair and makeup stand out in a subtle but important way. Light brows, strong eye looks, and unexpected color choices make everything feel a bit more surreal without taking attention away from the clothes.
Overall, it‚Äôs the kind of editorial that doesn‚Äôt try too hard to explain itself. It‚Äôs more about the mood, the visuals, and how everything comes together naturally.
Credits:
Photography: Tim Walker
Styling: Jacob Kjeldgaard
Model: Edie Campbell
Makeup: Miranda Joyce
Hair: Shon
Editorial: ‚ÄúCheck-Mate‚Äù, Vogue Italia, December 2015

posts.caption:
At Alessandro Michele‚Äôs Gucci FW2018 Cyborg show, during Milan Fashion Week, the runway was presented inside a ‚Äûhighly controlled‚Äú, clinical environment designed to resemble an operating theater. The set was defined by sterile green-turquoise walls, harsh overhead lighting, stainless surfaces, and hospital-style beds, creating a setting that felt detached from traditional fashion spectacle and closer to a staged experiment.
Within this environment, models moved at a measured pace through the space, each look constructed with extreme density and precision. The collection itself merged multiple visual languages at once: historical tailoring, ecclesiastical references, sportswear influences, and highly decorative elements were layered together in a way that resisted any single interpretation. Accessories and props extended the garments into sculptural forms, including hyper-realistic replicas of human heads and hybrid, creature-like objects that disrupted the boundary between body and object.
Beauty direction reinforced this constructed logic. Hair was sculptural and intentionally artificial in finish, while makeup emphasized transformation rather than enhancement, pushing the idea that identity here is something assembled rather than inherent.
Seen in hindsight, the collection aligns closely with contemporary conversations around identity as a layered, mediated construct, anticipating the visual and conceptual codes that now dominate digital fashion culture.
Credits :
Creative Director: Alessandro Michele
Casting Director: Maida Gregori Boina
Set Design: Gideon Ponte
Production Design: Gucci ArtLab / Gucci Creative Team
Makeup: Pat McGrath
Hair: Guido Palau
Special Effects / Props: Makinarium
Music: Steve Mackey
Runway Production: Art + Commerce / Gucci Team
Inspiration: Donna Haraway ‚Äì A Cyborg Manifesto
Runway Photography: Sonny Vandevelde / Indigital.tv

posts.caption:
SOPHIE, who passed away aged 34 in 2021, only released one official studio album in her lifetime, but there‚Äôs a whole treasure trove of unreleased projects, live sets, leaks, and deep lore. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
meg & Skip the co-founders of WHOLENEW.WORLD, cumulatively spent ‚Äúat least four full days‚Äù trawling through old Instagram tags, Discord channels, Facebook comments, forum posts, and DMing SOPHIE fans to assemble an exhaustive archive of material, often unearthed after years in someone‚Äôs camera roll. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Currently, the site hosts live sets and setlists dating back to a 2010 show at London‚Äôs XOYO (other highlights, which meg reels off like a SOPHIE encyclopedia, include her 2014 Just Jam livestream, her live debut at Teragram Ballroom in 2017, and her 2019 Unsound set). Here, its creators take us through a few of their favourite hidden gems.‚ÅÝ

The natural diamond industry isn‚Äôt declining. It‚Äôs being redefined. üíé
At a powerful roundtable during IIJS Bharat Signature 2026, industry leaders unpacked a hard truth:
This is no longer a cyclical slowdown. It‚Äôs a structural reset.
From shrinking market share and shifting consumer priorities to the rise of lab-grown diamonds and digital-first retail, the rules have changed.
But here‚Äôs where it gets interesting:
‚ú® Supply is tightening
‚ú® High-end demand is growing
‚ú® Consumers are still willing to spend just differently
The real challenge?
Rebuilding aspiration, relevance and emotional connection.
Because at its core, this industry has never been about product.
It‚Äôs always been about the dream.
If you‚Äôre in the diamond business, 2026 isn‚Äôt about waiting for recovery.
It‚Äôs about reinvention.
What do you think the industry needs most right now better branding or better pricing?
#DiamondWorld #NaturalDiamonds #DiamondIndustry #DWMagazine #RetailStrategy

posts.caption:
Predator turned born-again British MAGA stan Russell Brand is under fire (again) after casually admitting on Megyn Kelly‚Äôs podcast that he slept with a 16-year-old.
‚ÄúThe plain fact of it is that in Europe and in the United Kingdom, where I‚Äôm from, the age of consent is 16, and I did sleep with a 16-year-old when I was 30,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúBut when I was 30, I was a very different person. I was a lot younger, and I was an immature 30-year-old.‚Äù ü§Æ
Kelly also raised new sexual assault allegations involving his ex-wife Katy Perry by Ruby Rose, which the singer has denied. ‚ÄúThis is probably the old school man in me... I don‚Äôt even hear the crime there.‚Äù
Brand, 50, is currently facing seven rape and sexual assault charges involving seven women tied to alleged incidents between 1999 and 2009. He has denied all charges.
Meanwhile, he‚Äôs spent recent years seemingly aiming for a redemption arc by speaking at multiple Turning Point USA events, cozying up to MAGAts like Dr. Oz and Worm Brain Jr., even receiving a ‚ÄúGlobal Defender of Freedom‚Äù award at Mar-a-Lago last year.
Nothing says moral rebirth quite like rebranding before trial, which begins in October.
#news #trial #threads #reels #fyp

posts.caption:
‚ÄúWho is the Bleu de Chanel man?‚Äù Chanel asked yesterday on IG, as a silhouetted figure of their new fragrance ambassador appeared on their feed.
Well, it definitely wasn‚Äôt Timoth√©e Chalamet, who took on the Bleu de Chanel ambassador role in 2023 after the fragrance‚Äôs first ever ambassador, Gaspard Ulliel. Ulliel had held the role for twelve years from Bleu de Chanel‚Äôs release in 2010 to his tragic death in 2022.
This morning, it was confirmed that Jacob Elordi would replace Chalamet. The announcement features various shots of the actor in profile, highlighting his famously sharp jawline and shaggy mullet. The clip ends on a shot of him pointing straight into the camera with two fingers. In other words, it‚Äôs highly masculine, authoritative, and a bit playful in contrast to the minimalistic and soft announcement for Chalamet just a few years ago.
It makes sense: Elordi has built an illustrious reputation that transcends his breakout TV role in Euphoria. He has an Oscar nom under his belt, has starred alongside Margot Robbie, who is also a Chanel ambassador and was featured with Elordi in an ad for Chanel No. 5 last year. Chanel describes him as ‚Äúa deeply determined man, driven by his instinct.‚Äù
For reference, Chalamet‚Äôs announcement in 2023 was much less specific, stating only that Bleu was ‚Äúa fragrance for the man who is deeply himself.‚Äù Perhaps that was Chalamet‚Äôs selling point at the time. Then again, perhaps that was Bleu de Chanel‚Äôs selling point at the time. What changed?
Since Chalamet‚Äôs appointment at Chanel, he graduated from the boyish, French-ish heartthrob with the pretty loose curls and the gender-bending outfits, becoming instead a devil-may-care troublemaker with a taste for hype styling and awkward facial hair. And, the charm that made him beloved in his early career all but wore off completely when he made incendiary comments about opera and ballet last February. Chanel, one Threads user notes, is a ‚Äúmajor patron‚Äù of the Paris Opera Ballet. Could these be related?
Further, will two or three stints be the norm for Chanel‚Äôs fragrance ambassadorships? Or can Ulliel‚Äôs longstanding role be repeated with Elordi?
In any case, Congrats Jacob!

Diet PRADA'.

posts.caption:
The Devil Wears Prada 2 press tour was packed with some of Anne Hathaway and Meryl Streep‚Äôs most fabulous fashion moments to date, culminating in an ultra chic NYC premier earlier this week. But the big DWP news now is that Sydney Sweeney was axed from the film.
In the long awaited sequel, Runway magazine is in trouble. So, Miranda Preistley must bring together her trusty team to get things back in order. That team includes her right-hand man Nigel Kipling, her former assistant Andy Sachs, and, lastly, Emily Charlton, who is now the head of Dior US. In the cut scene, Nigel, Andy, and Miranda would have descended upon the Dior offices to enlist Emily‚Äôs help, only to find her knee-deep in a fitting with a celebrity. That celebrity would have been Sydney Sweeney as herself.
Entertainment Weekly reports that the scene was edited out because it ‚Äúdid not work structurally.‚Äù But, the decision to nix Sweeney comes at a pretty coincidental moment. She is still in hot water after her eugenics-ridden American Eagle ad last year, and has only leaned into the controversy in subsequent interviews. Since then, she has courted more controversy gleefully, staging a faux stunt at the Hollywood sign to promote her lingerie line and playing a provocative role in Euphoria season 3. Her actions have been lauded by Trump and she appears to have become an unofficial symbol of ideal femininity for conservative white-supremacist men.
Even before the news of her cut DWP2 scene, audiences have been speculating on the toxicity of Sweeney‚Äôs brand. The actor often wears vintage pieces on red carpets, causing fashion insiders to believe that brands refuse to dress her. Others called for the cancellation of Euphoria after Sweeney appeared in a pornographic baby outfit.
It‚Äôs a little hard to believe that Sweeney was cut from DWP2 because of structural issues. It‚Äôs not like casting Sweeney, who reportedly makes millions for on-screen appearances these days, is a throwaway decision. Then again, we are talking about Runway mag, where Miranda Preistley once canceled the autumn jacket shoot to pull up the Sedona shoot. And we all know how much THAT cost.

Trump's favorite leading lady will not appear in the sequel amid a rotating door of scandals. @brinzaraa THE EVIL HAS BEEN DEFEATED Diet PRADA'.

posts.caption:
The fashions seen over the last two Coachella weekends were far from the eccentric, highly curated, culturally-appropriative bohemian glam looks we expected to see. In fact, they were the total opposite.
Stars like Kendall Jenner, Hailey Baldwin, Amber Rose, and Vanessa Hudgens wrote the playbook for how to dress at Coachella: Feathers in your hair, tribal headdresses and flower crowns, macrame crop-tops, and Navajo prints. Coachella style was deeply problematic, it was plastered all over Tumblr, and it was so codified that it became its own retail category: the annual ‚Äúfestival collection.‚Äù
But now, the same celebs are turning against it. Kendall Jenner was seen this year in basic tank tops and baggy jorts. Katy Perry and Justin Trudeau‚Äôs white t-shirt stunned the internet. And Hailey Beiber, the first lady of Beiberchella, wore only a vintage windbreaker one weekend, and a hoodie with running shorts the next weekend.
There is a lot influencing this: We spotted indie sleaze style reflected in Gabbriette‚Äôs Chrome Hearts looks. Amber Rose‚Äôs shorts, Hailey Bieber‚Äôs and Emma Chamberlain‚Äôs leather boots, and Amelia Gray‚Äôs stiff straight-cut denim all echoed fashion‚Äôs growing interest in workwear. And, the quiet luxury movement informed almost all these outfits being that each came with surreptitious designer tags.
But also, this movement toward underdressing took place under the giant YouTube screen of Beiberchella, which was, by the way, similarly minimal and ad hoc. Erego, this isn‚Äôt just a fashion issue (it never is!) but is indicative of a larger trend that is skeptical of overproduction and is nostalgic for authenticity. Perhaps it‚Äôs a foil to the onslaught of product placements, highly styled appearances, and manufactured moments that have overtaken Coachella.
Celebrity underdressers appear to be offering counterpoint, a way to distance themselves from the masses who are clawing for visibility at music fests. Or perhaps they‚Äôre reaching for the styles that made music festivals so iconic in the first place, back when it was common to be a little messy. Dirty, even. A la Kate Moss at Glastonbury. Is Coachella underdressing actually a breath of fresh air?
posts.alt_text: Photo by Diet Prada ‚Ñ¢ on April 20, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'RIP Overdressing at Coachella Celebrities and seasoned Coachella-goers embraced underdressing in contrast to their styling in former years. Diet PRADA'.

posts.caption:
Hallmark Channel dropped the promo poster for its new movie ‚ÄòAll‚Äôs Fair in Love and Mahjong‚Äô on Friday and the internet wasted no time dragging it.
For a movie centered around mahjong, people are side-eyeing the casting choices. The poster shows a mostly white cast, with just one Asian actor, Yan-Kay Crystal Lowe, who is of Chinese-Scottish descent.
The rom-com synopsis reads: ‚ÄúA school nurse turns her love of the game Mahjong into a new path, inspired by a jovial contractor fixing up her home, who encourages her to take a chance on the future.‚Äù
Meanwhile, the mahjong tiles featured on the poster are from the film‚Äôs branded set, which Hallmark plans to sell on its website through a collab with Miss Mahjong. The Kansas-based game company says it was founded by ‚Äúa trio of Mahjong-obsessed friends and neighbors‚Äù named Anne Marie, Morgan, and Betsy. Although the founders don‚Äôt share additional details (üßê) the optics are giving the same energy as a the whitewashed mahjong brands we previously called out: Oh My Mahjong! (just three weeks ago) and The Mahjong Line back in 2021.
The film‚Äôs title, a spin on the phrase, ‚Äúall‚Äôs fair in love and war,‚Äù was probably meant to be cheeky, but instead the comments section seems ready for battle with the network.
‚ÄúJust do monopoly next time or something,‚Äù wrote one person. ‚ÄúCollective ancestral sigh,‚Äù wrote another.
To top it off, the movie is set to premiere May 9‚Ä¶ during AAPI Heritage Month. @hallmarkchannel it‚Äôs not too late to scrap this mess.
#mahjong #movie #film #aapi #viral

posts.caption:
Babe, wake up! Ranveer has designed his own adidas Originals Superstar. Superstar for THEE superstar!!
A Ranveer Singh for adidas Originals sneaker feels like a long time coming. The RGB colour-way track-sets, the bucket hats, the special collabs, and the plethora of shoes. Ranveer‚Äôs wardrobe choice and the Originals identity have merged over time to create a streetwear aesthetic that is instantly recognisable. He has sported a style that no one in his level of fame dares to tread, and the brand has served as a cultural lightning rod, bringing to him the best of global streetwear.
So finally when he gets to be a part of adidas India‚Äôs first ever statement shoe drop, it seems like a no-brainer. A MAJOR choice if you may. To collab at this level with Originals is not just an acknowledgment of Ranveer‚Äôs love for the brand, but also of his influence. Not influencer, baby, INFLUENTIAL. His ‚Äòsuperstar‚Äô-dom, if you may. As they say, strike the iron while it‚Äôs hot, and adidas Originals has clearly clocked it, because if anyone can sell a shoe right now, it‚Äôs HIM.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There are artists who arrive with noise, and then there are artists like Zayn Malik (@zayn), the kind who never really needed to raise his voice to command attention. Some people have presence; Malik has always had pull. Fifteen years after his debut, and nearly a decade into his solo career, Malik returns to ELLE India, where he first made history in 2018 with his first-ever Indian magazine cover. There‚Äôs no forced ‚Äúfull circle‚Äù narrative here, no overworked nostalgia. If anything, this moment feels less like a return and more like a continuation ‚Äî the same artist, only more settled into himself. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt feel like I‚Äôm at such a different place in my career,‚Äù he says. ‚ÄúI feel like I‚Äôm at a different place and point as a human; I‚Äôm developing, growing, and learning from life experiences.‚Äù That distinction feels important. The artistry was never in question. If anything, it is the life around it that now feels more fully aligned. Head to the üîóin bio to read the full conversation with our cover star.

posts.caption:
@oliviadeano is everywhere‚Äîand for good reason.
Since the September release of her hit sophomore album, ‚ÄòThe Art of Loving,‚Äô the British songstress has been riding a serious wave of momentum on both sides of the pond. Add in a Grammy win, standout live performances, a @cartier campaign, and critical raves, and it‚Äôs clear: Dean‚Äôs starpower is undeniable. As she prepares to kick off the album‚Äôs sold-out world tour, Dean is trying her best to soak it all in‚Äîand define success on her own terms.
In ELLE‚Äôs Women in Music cover story, @sylviaobell chats with the Grammy winner about navigating public opinion in her newfound spotlight, the wisdom the women in her life have imparted on her, and the precedent she hopes to set with her upcoming tour: ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt think you need to be someone who‚Äôs got loads of money to enjoy your favorite album.‚Äù Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
‚ÄúHi Shakaila we‚Äôre xyz producers and we‚Äôd love to make a tv show with you‚Äù I‚Äôve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from ü§© to ü´©
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that‚Äôs what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself üòÖ
This black history month especially, I‚Äôm inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it‚Äôs possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That‚Äôs why I‚Äôm so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

posts.caption:
California released Amazon‚Äôs internal emails on Monday. They are worth reading carefully if you are a fashion brand that sells on the platform.
The short version: Amazon identified products listed at lower prices on Walmart and Target, contacted its vendors, and asked them to get those prices raised. The vendors complied. Levi Strauss confirmed Walmart had raised the price of its khaki trousers to $29.99 the same day Amazon asked. Hanes contacted both Walmart and Target directly after Amazon sent it links to cheaper competitor listings. California Attorney General Rob Bonta is calling this price-fixing. Read more in this column by Editor-in-Chief Anuj Kumar on fashionlawjournal.com

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands üéâ¬ÝThis is a major accomplishment, as even more brands‚Äîfrom the small and indie through the large and luxury‚Äîare in our Directory.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
What goes into ratings? ü§î A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (‚ÄúWe Avoid‚Äù) to our highest score (‚ÄúGreat‚Äù). Ratings are based solely on a brand‚Äôs transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
How do we select brands to rate? Well‚Ä¶with your help ü•π Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, ‚ÄúGood‚Äù and ‚ÄúGreat‚Äù. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means‚Ä¶6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Here‚Äôs to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers ü•Ç Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ‚ú®‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion‚ÅÝ

posts.caption:
Garment workers need our help by advocating further for change in fashion üíî‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
For many workers throughout fashion‚Äôs supply chain, there is no guarantee of safe working conditions, proper payment, or benefits, all of which takes advantage of often vulnerable people as the biggest brand seek profits at the expense of persons they continue to take advantage of üòÝ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
A key way workers can create change is through collective bargaining, which allows workers and their employers to negotiate around their conditions. This is a fundamental element of autonomy‚Äîbut not all brands are working to ensure this is the case, as many enable a problematic status quo.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
üö® This is where you come in: by speaking up to brands and local governments, maintaining pressure to change the system, we can ensure workers worldwide are getting their due. Swipe to learn about this problem and how you can help. Explore the full story at the link ‚Üë‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
#Fashion #SHEINGals #Zara #TemuFinds #SustainableFashion‚ÅÝ

‚ÄòWhen I tell people I live with my parents, there‚Äôs a look I can‚Äôt quite place: equal parts horror and fascination, as well as the unspoken question: why would you do that to yourself?‚Äô
When Alison Taylor realised that her parents were going to need increasing amounts of help, she and her husband Si decided to build them an annexe at the end of their garden - moving the couple into their home alongside their three primary school-aged children while it was being built. posts.caption: Michaela Coel isn‚Äôt method dressing‚Äîshe's her own character. Our March cover star is in the midst of two press tours happening simultaneously. posts.caption: Ten years on, @Beyonce‚Äôs 2016‚Äôs "Lemonade" is still a masterpiece, and a win for modern fashion. Every fur coat, hoodie, Gucci monogram set, pinafore, and turn-of-the-century lace bodysuit said something important. Her clothing choices underscored that idea of embracing unbridled, unapologetic confidence. But the mustard-colored, tiered ruffle gown from @roberto_cavalli worn in the ‚ÄúHold Up‚Äù music video did something different; it is arguably one of the most impactful revenge dresses. ‚ÄúShe always transforms the clothes into something much bigger,‚Äù @Peter_Dundas, Cavalli‚Äôs creative director at the time, shared. At the link in bio, we reflect on the album and examine the deeper meaning behind her style. Photographed by @campbelladdy, September 2021 (Slide 2).

posts.caption:
@uniformdisplay takes the @alphaindustries CWU-45 and strips it of its rigid context, rebuilding it as something far more personal. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
At the heart of the collaboration is the CWU-45, a jacket long rooted in military function. Anchored in the idea of ‚ÄúNOT JUST UNIFORM,‚Äù the collaboration pushes past the notion of uniformity as instruction, treating it instead as raw material, something to be interpreted, bent, and worn as self-expression. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ

posts.caption:
At Milan Design Week, fashion brands dress everything except the runway.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
This year, the real style flex comes from staff uniforms. Yohji Yamamoto teamed up with @aesop to create oversized, all-black jackets via his Y‚Äôs line, complete with crinkled ‚Äúvintage‚Äù texture ‚Äî luxury outerwear disguised as workwear. Meanwhile, LVMH Prize winner Soshiotsuki swapped tailoring for velour @asics tracksuits, blending retro suiting codes with athletic silhouettes in a sharp, cropped navy set.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Both projects blur the line between uniform and collectible fashion, making everyday staff look like they‚Äôve stepped out of a concept store campaign. If anything, they leave you wondering why these aren‚Äôt full collaborations ‚Äî because honestly, who wouldn‚Äôt want in?
posts.alt_text: Photo by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on April 24, 2026. May be an image of fleece, sportswear, jacket and text that says 'ý∂ß THE MOST UNDERRATED THING AT MILAN DESIGN WEEK? TH STAFF UNIFORMS H'.

posts.caption:
@stoneisland distills decades of innovation into one sharp idea: one jacket, six identities.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
The new chapter of NO SEASONS, a concept first introduced by Massimo Osti in 1989, centers on a single outerwear silhouette reimagined through six signature fabrics: Tela Resinata, Nylon Prismatico-TC, Raso Gommato, David-TC, Crinkle Reps NY, and Panno. Each version is dyed in piombo, letting the character of each material subtly shift tone, texture, and depth while keeping the design constant.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
The result is a study in material science and construction, where fabric leads and form follows. The installation is open to the public from April 20 to 26, 10AM‚Äì8PM, at Capsule Plaza, Via Achille Maiocchi 8, Milan.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on April 24, 2026. May be an image of goosedown coat, jacket, parka and text that says '„Éî ·àú Â±ï ÌïôÎùº STONE ISLAND K THIS ONE JACKET AND MADE IT IN SIX SIGNATURE FABRICS H'.

posts.caption:
What happens when you use a material famous for being as light as air to recreate a slab of solid rock? Meet ‚ÄúAirstone.‚Äù
Now on view and available to shop at Via Santa Marta 10 in Milan‚Äôs 5VIE district, the new Airstone tote bag is a design paradox.
Designed by @nichettostudio, the bag‚Äôs form was originally hand-carved from a block of rock before being 3D-scanned and injected with @XLEXTRALIGHT technology.
The result? A primitive-futuristic, limited-edition accessory that looks like a heavy, jagged artifact but weighs next to nothing. It‚Äôs a sensory mind-game that challenges how we perceive mass and material memory.
Sustainability isn‚Äôt an afterthought here, either.
The bag is crafted using XL EXTRALIGHT¬Æ Sustainable+, a blend featuring 51% post-industrial waste.
To launch the Airstone, XL EXTRALIGHT¬Æ has opened a popup installation which mixes acid-yellow accents, industrial metal cages, and sculptural foam rocks. It‚Äôll have you wondering whether you‚Äôre in a high-end boutique or a geology lab.
The Airstone tote is available in three colorways exclusively at the Milan Design Week pop-up at Via Santa Marta 10.

posts.caption:

‚ÅÝ
The look mimics notebook paper through clever stitching ‚Äî reddish-pink vertical lines paired with light blue horizontal ones ‚Äî brought to life using layered white T-shirt fabrics, some faded or yellowed to evoke aged pages.‚ÅÝ

Originally made two years ago, the piece is being revisited to highlight its concept and construction, offering a simple but inventive idea that others might build on or reinterpret.

posts.caption:
Man of the Moment ‚Äî Yashasvi Jaiswal (@yashasvijaiswal28) continues to rise, and we‚Äôre here for every second of it.
From dreaming of centuries in blue to turning those visions into reality and now hitting it out of the park as the opener for Rajasthan Royal (@rajasthanroyals) in IPL, his journey is the very definition of hard work meeting opportunity. Powered as much by an unshakable mindset as by his brilliance on the pitch, our cover star opens up about the drive, discipline, and hunger that fuel his remarkable ascent, ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know where I‚Äôm going to go, but I want to enjoy the journey.‚Äù
Starting out as a cricketing prodigy, now 24, he‚Äôs all about keeping his head in the game, stopping every now and then, to savour the simple joys of life. And maybe that is the secret sauce to success, one game at a time.
Here‚Äôs raising a toast to the ‚Äòplayer of the match‚Äô- Yashasvi Jaiswal!

posts.caption:
Gen Z did not invent the messy breakup but they did build a whole vocabulary around surviving it. The situationship that had its moments but no label, the soft launch that never became official, the slow fade you kept making excuses for, the almost relationship that somehow left the biggest gap because it never existed enough to grieve properly. Categorising it does not fix it but it does make the 3am spiral slightly more organised. This is the glossary nobody gave us.

posts.caption:
Pilates at 8, brunch at 10, errands that somehow turn into a whole afternoon, and a last minute plan you said yes to before checking what you were wearing. Sporty chic in 2026 was built for exactly this schedule. The wellness era did not just change how we move, it changed what we wear doing everything else.
Streamlined sets, elevated basics, the kind of athleisure that lives in the grey area between gym kit and actual fashion and somehow wins both. Your wardrobe should be able to keep up with your schedule.

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It‚Äôs been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‚ÄòAlmost Famous,‚Äô and she‚Äôs been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success‚Äîand her roots in Hollywood royalty‚Äîshe‚Äôs remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
‚ÄúI just really want to make movies that people love,‚Äù Hudson tells L‚ÄôOFFICIEL. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.‚Äù
That, of course, has always been Hudson‚Äôs strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‚ÄòSong Sung Blue,‚Äô she‚Äôs now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‚ÄòRunning Point.‚Äô In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson‚Äôs own upbringing, she‚Äôs quick to dismiss that narrative. ‚ÄúJeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,‚Äù she says. ‚ÄúFor me, we‚Äôre just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they‚Äôre not.‚Äù
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she‚Äôs still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn‚Äôt point to accolades‚Äîjust a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
‚ÄúI enter every experience like it‚Äôs new, like I don‚Äôt know how it‚Äôs going to unfold,‚Äù she explains. ‚ÄúI come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I‚Äôm there, I can be flexible, not rigid.‚Äù Read Hudson‚Äôs full interview at the link in our bio. ‚ÅÝ

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It‚Äôs been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‚ÄòAlmost Famous,‚Äô and she‚Äôs been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success‚Äîand her roots in Hollywood royalty‚Äîshe‚Äôs remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
‚ÄúI just really want to make movies that people love,‚Äù Hudson tells L‚ÄôOFFICIEL. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.‚Äù
That, of course, has always been Hudson‚Äôs strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‚ÄòSong Sung Blue,‚Äô she‚Äôs now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‚ÄòRunning Point.‚Äô In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson‚Äôs own upbringing, she‚Äôs quick to dismiss that narrative. ‚ÄúJeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,‚Äù she says. ‚ÄúFor me, we‚Äôre just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they‚Äôre not.‚Äù
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

Qui la moda si apre al dialogo, diventando linguaggio culturale in un ambiente in cui arte e bellezza convivono e si trasformano, restituendo una dimensione viva, condivisa e configurando una nuova retorica della moda.

posts.caption:
‚ÅÝWhile her approach to acting has always been instinctive and fluid, producing calls for a more structured mindset for @katehudson, who continues to expand her role behind the scenes as executive producer on ‚ÄòRunning Point.‚Äô ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
It‚Äôs also a natural next step in her career. Inspired by her mother, Goldie Hawn‚Äîone of the first actresses to produce her own films‚ÄîHudson explored producing early on, but stepped back after ‚ÄòBride Wars,‚Äô when she was a new mom and the timing didn‚Äôt feel right.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
As with everything else, she waited until she could fully commit‚Äîa quality that sets her up for a successful future as a producer. Read our full interview with Hudson at the link in our bio. ‚ÅÝ

posts.caption:
Bridgerton isn‚Äôt just a romantic period drama, it‚Äôs one of Netflix‚Äôs most lavish productions. The series reportedly carries a staggering budget of around $168 million, placing it among the most expensive shows created by the streaming giant.
Each episode is estimated to cost around $7 million, funding the show‚Äôs extravagant costumes, grand sets, and cinematic storytelling that recreates Regency-era London. The scale of production is what gives the series its signature opulence and visual richness.
The spending doesn‚Äôt stop at sets and wardrobes. Reports also reveal impressive cast salaries, with some actors and voice talents earning up to $1 million for their roles, reflecting the show‚Äôs massive global success.
From lavish balls to royal romances, Bridgerton proves that luxury storytelling comes with a royal price tag and Netflix is willing to pay for it.
Images: Courtesy of Netflix
#Netflix #Luxurious

posts.caption:
Indian cricketer Jasprit Bumrah was recently seen wearing the ‚Çπ36-lakh @JacobAndCo Epic X ‚ÄòSher-e-Punjab‚Äô Edition, a distinctive timepiece that blends high-end watchmaking with strong cultural inspiration.
Created by Jacob & Co as a tribute to Sikh heritage, the watch features a lightweight 44mm titanium case and a skeletonised dial that reveals the intricate mechanics within. The vertical bridges are engraved with meaningful Sikh symbols, including the lion representing courage and leadership, the Khanda symbolising strength and unity, and the sacred Ek Onkar reflecting the belief in one universal God.
The titanium structure is paired with a bold blue honeycomb-textured rubber strap, a colour closely associated with Sikh identity. At its core is the hand-wound JCAM02 movement, offering a 48-hour power reserve while showcasing the watch‚Äôs detailed inner workings through the open dial.
Limited to just 50 pieces worldwide, the Sher-e-Punjab Edition honours the legacy of Guru Gobind Singh and the founding of the Khalsa, making it both a luxury watch and a tribute to heritage and resilience. ‚åö‚ú®

posts.caption:
Formula One is officially set to return to Istanbul Park from 2027, after Turkey secured a long-term agreement to host the race for at least five consecutive years. The deal will see the circuit remain on the calendar through the 2031 season, marking a major comeback for one of the sport‚Äôs most popular modern tracks.
The announcement was made during a high-profile event attended by Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan, Stefano Domenicali, CEO of Formula One, and Mohammed Ben Sulayem, President of the FIA. The return follows years of negotiations after the circuit last hosted a race in 2021, when it briefly reappeared on the calendar.
Known for its technical 14-turn layout and the iconic high-speed Turn 8, Istanbul Park has long been considered one of the most challenging and exciting circuits for drivers and fans alike. Its comeback also reflects Formula One‚Äôs strategy to strengthen its presence in key global markets while maintaining a balanced calendar capped at 24 races.
Overall, the return of the Turkish Grand Prix signals a renewed commitment to expanding Formula One‚Äôs global footprint, while bringing one of the sport‚Äôs fan-favorite venues back into regular rotation after several years away.
@f1 #Luxurious

She paired the historic jewel with a traditional Banarasi saree from Swadesh, her initiative supporting Indian artisans, along with a custom blouse by Manish Malhotra. posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
From engineered gym run-ins to a romance that unfolded at its own pace, Allu Sirish (@allusirish) and Nayanika Reddy‚Äôs (@nayanika_reddy) story feels less like a whirlwind and more like a rhythm built over time.
‚ÄúIt wasn‚Äôt like we suddenly started going on back-to-back dates. It was more of a slow-burn romance,‚Äù says Sirish, recalling how a series of chance meetings ‚Äî and one very intentional gym switch ‚Äî finally brought them together.
For Nayanika, the turning point was simpler.
‚ÄúI realised how easy it felt to be around him. Our conversations were natural, and there was always a sense of comfort. Over time, it became clear that we were building something meaningful,‚Äù she shares.
Just days before their wedding, the couple comes together for their first editorial shoot, speaking to Manifest about love that grew organically, families that were always one step ahead, and why the most meaningful relationships don‚Äôt need grand gestures to feel certain.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

posts.caption:
#ManifestDigitalCover | The Shefali Shah Effect
For over three decades, Shefali Shah (@shefalishahofficial) has built a career on instinct, honesty, and an unwavering refusal to fit into boxes. From the unforgettable Pyari in Satya to the formidable DCP Vartika Chaturvedi in Delhi Crime, her performances have never relied on noise ‚Äî only truth.
‚ÄúA few years ago, if someone told me a woman in her mid-forties would lead a show, I would have laughed. And if they said it would be me, I would have laughed louder,‚Äù she says.
In our latest digital cover, the actor reflects on evolving with time, finding freedom in flawed characters, 25 years of marriage, and why success today means something far quieter than fame.

posts.caption:
This month, Vogue did something it had never done before, something most people thought it would probably never do: It put two 76-year-old women on its cover. Sure, they weren‚Äôt just any 76-year-olds. They were Meryl Streep and the Vogue overseer Anna Wintour, and they were there in a meta way to discuss the mythology around ‚ÄúThe Devil Wears Prada 2,‚Äù in which Streep plays a version of Wintour. But still, 76.
The most recent round of fashion shows, which our fashion critic Vanessa Friedman attended last month, were notable not only for the almost total lack of size diversity on the runway, but also for the fact that they were, at the same time, taking one giant step forward when it came to another aspect of inclusivity: Age.
The Chanel show opened with 50-year-old Stephanie Cavalli, who was one of 15 models over 40 on its runway. Bottega Veneta had nine older models. Tom Ford, nine (women and men). Givenchy, eight. Balenciaga, five. Louis Vuitton, four. Representation of women of a certain age has never been higher. What‚Äôs going on and why now? At the link in our bio, @vvfriedman delves into the striking shift.

But you can head off a lot of the stress by sitting down with your partner early on and coming up with a clear vision of what you want. Identifying your priorities, desires and budget before diving into the details will simplify the process and keep you on track. To help you navigate these decisions, the experts at The New York Times Wedding section created a quiz to determine what kind of wedding is the best fit for you. posts.caption:
At a starry premiere for "The Devil Wears Prada 2," fashion industry figures reflected on just how much the original film got right.
The movie, released in 2006, starred Anne Hathaway as the blunder-prone assistant to Miranda Priestly, a fashion magazine editor played by Meryl Streep who has a gaze withering enough to penetrate the darkest of sunglasses. Now, the sequel considers how their dynamic ‚Äî and the media landscape they occupy ‚Äî has shifted after two decades.
At the film‚Äôs premiere in Manhattan on Monday night, Hathaway and Streep posed with their co-stars Stanley Tucci and Emily Blunt on a red carpet outside Lincoln Center. Also present were the real-life inspirations, Vogue's Anna Wintour and the head of editorial content for American Vogue, Chloe Malle.
Inside David Geffen Hall, industry figures reflected on how much the movie had in common with the world it portrays. Tap the link in our bio for highlights from the premiere. Visuals by @apisukh

posts.caption:
At Coachella last Friday afternoon, fans rushed to claim a spot in front of one of the festival stages. But the crowd wasn‚Äôt camping out for that night‚Äôs headliner, Sabrina Carpenter, or for the buzzy girl group Katseye. Attendees were patiently waiting to see Bob Baker Marionette Theater, home to some of Los Angeles‚Äôs most popular and beloved puppets.
Started in 1963 by a duo of puppeteers, Bob Baker and Alton Wood, the institution is one of the largest and longest-running puppet theaters in the U.S. But the theater has faced the prospect of closure several times over the past few decades, so performing at Coachella was a major moment for the group.
Mary Fagot, co-executive director of the theater, said that joining the festival lineup felt natural. ‚ÄúWe were like, Of course you‚Äôre reaching out to us,‚Äù Fagot said with a laugh. ‚ÄúCoachella needs us to bring the real and the connected and the joy and the fun and the sense of not taking yourself too seriously.‚Äù
Get a closer look at the puppets' Coachella debut and learn more about Bob Baker Marionette Theater at the link in our bio. Visuals by @ye.fan and @dontgabalot

posts.caption:
This week, not only did #Zendaya rewear her 2016 Oscars #VivienneWestwood gown, she also borrowed a Armani dress previously worn by the queen of outfit repetion, #CateBlanchett. It makes you think, should this become more common?
Bound by contracts, loyalty and evolving trends, celebrities have a responsibility to showcase new designers and support the fashion industry. Red carpets function as global advertising platforms providing exposure to talent, but should more celebs rewear their old clothes? By rewearing, new ways of styling and tailoring can transform an existing piece into something nostalgic yet completely new.
Most celebs often wear custom looks that are loaned to them ensuring each appearance feels exclusive and newsworthy. But once photographed and discussed, these garments (which take hundreds of hours to create) are archived. Yet in an increasingly sustainability-conscious landscape, should this change?
What do you think?
Images courtesy of Getty & Backgrid

posts.caption:
They say you can tell when a designer loves the women they‚Äôre dressing, and that‚Äôs pretty obvious with #SarahBurton‚Äôs #Givenchy. Her latest collection moved between tailoring and draping, a reflection of the modern women she is designing for.
One of the most striking looks was the floral dress, an adaptation of a painting by Filipino artist Olan Ventura. Featuring cascading strands of fringe which distorted the floral motifs across the dress so they appeared almost blurred or melting.
The headpieces, by @stephenjonesmillinery , were actually Silk T-shirts twisted and wrapped around the models‚Äô heads, forming sculptural shapes reminiscent of Dutch Golden Age portraiture, specifically Rogier van der Weyden‚Äôs Woman with a Winged Bonnet.
What did you think of the collection?

posts.caption:
#Dior has been making some excellent red carpet looks recently and I can‚Äôt help but notice @jonathan.anderson‚Äôs revival of the Victorian bustle.
The bustle emerged in the late 19th century as a padded or structured undergarment worn beneath skirts to create volume at the back of the body, emphasizing a narrow waist. They relied on layers of fabric, pleating, and internal frameworks. Aptly linking #ChristianDior‚Äôs 1947 ‚ÄúNew Look,‚Äù emphasising a cinched waist and structured hips.
#JonathanAnderson‚Äôs reinterpretation focuses on sculptural volume, creating shapes through draped panels, bows, or layered fabrics, creating extremely sculptural and romantic silhouettes. We‚Äôve seen this in gowns, skirts, tailoring, and shorts, featuring pleated or structured volumes attached to the back of garments, expanding the concept of the bustle beyond traditional #HauteCouture.
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on March 16, 2026. May be an image of magazine, gown, poster and text that says 'The Bustle Is Back TheBustleIsBackat at Jonathan Anderson's Dior'.

posts.caption:
Could #Madonna‚Äôs stolen 2006 #Gucci jacket by #FridaGiannini spark a quiet revival? We all know and love #TomFord‚Äôs Gucci, but at this point it‚Äôs been overdone. Could Giannini‚Äôs era be the next interesting Gucci archive for true fashion girlies to pull from?
Frida had a difficult role stepping in after Ford, yet her version of Gucci brought stability and a different kind of glamour. This was the Gucci I grew up with and she made the brand feel polished and aspirational. A major part of her impact came through accessories, strengthening Gucci‚Äôs position as a leader in handbags and leather goods.
She loved jewel tones and metallics, and her references to the 1970s set the groundwork for what would come from #AlessandroMichele. While Michele pushed those references into maximalism and eccentricity, Frida‚Äôs work feels more archival than theatrical.
What did you think of Frida‚Äôs @gucci ?

posts.caption:
Seeing #ElsaSchiaparelli‚Äôs archive of perfumes at the @vamuseum made me realise‚Ä¶why are (most) luxury perfume bottles so boring now?
#Schiaparelli worked with creative collaborators such as Jean-Michel Frank, #SalvadorDal√≠, Leonor Fini, and Marcel Vert√®s to produce bottles and advertisements that were playful, imaginative, and sometimes shocking. Her most famous perfume, Shocking (1937), came in a bottle shaped like a woman‚Äôs torso, inspired by #MaeWest. Another example is Snuff, her only men‚Äôs perfume, designed in the shape of a pipe (see slide 11)
Today #perfume bottles today feel so minimal, focusing on consistent, recognisable designs. I guess clean, simple bottles are easier to produce, ship, and display, while complex shapes are more expensive to manufacture and more fragile to handle at scale. Culturally, contemporary luxury marketing often prioritises advertising and celebrity endorsements, shifting creativity away from the bottle itself.

posts.caption:
Remember that viral #Loewe tomato bag? (Shoutout @conndo) Well, it seems #JonathanAnderson‚Äôs love for fruit and vegetables has left a lasting impression on Loewe‚Äôs designs. Their latest ¬£4,000 leather artichoke bag makes you wonder, why are they so obsessed with fruit and vegetables?
These items aren‚Äôt just gimmicks; they‚Äôre demonstrations of Loewe‚Äôs craftsmanship, requiring intricate pattern cutting, moulding, and a high level of attention to detail. It also pays homage to the brand‚Äôs Spanish roots, drawing on a Mediterranean aesthetic of fresh produce and wellness. It aligns with a more relaxed, lifestyle-driven idea of luxury.
There‚Äôs also a sense of surrealism, where ordinary objects are elevated. That‚Äôs why these pieces feel more like wearable art than conventional accessories. Would you buy your 5 a day from @loewe ?

posts.caption:
#Opinion | Standup comedian Kaneez Surka @kaneezsurka recently opened up about the "beauty of divorce" on a podcast by Aditi Chandanani @therapywith_aditivc. "Oh you MUST get divorced!" Kaneez quipped, getting Aditi all excited about 'life 2.0'. "When you get divorced, society just drops all expectations of you... they give up on you, and the giving up is the best part!" Kaneez expressed.
Divorce used to be a woman's cue to hide way from society; a label that crept into every introduction. She is just a woman anymore; she is a divorcee. But the women of 2026 are rewriting what it means to walk away from an unfulfilling marriage. They are refusing to carry endings like shame, turning divorce into a cultural statement. Earlier, divorce defined the woman. Now, the woman defines the divorce.
A woman who leaves an unhappy marriage disrupts a comforting myth - that self sacrifice is the highest form of femininity. When she says, ‚ÄúThis is not working for me,‚Äù she exposes how many others are quietly tolerating what does not work for them either. And that can feel threatening to many.  But here is the truth women know in their bones. Divorce doesn't celebrate heartbreak; it is usually the aftermath of it. So why should women carry the shame of something that often requires immense courage? Why should they be the ones apologising for choosing peace?
Read the full article at www.shethepeople.tv | Views expressed by the author are their own.

posts.caption:
Throughout history, women have made various efforts to make this world more just and sustainable. However, their initiatives were always treated as of secondary importance and did not receive much public recognition and support as they deserved.
India, a country which is known for its diversity in many aspects, including flora and fauna, has seen many women-led environmental movements. Indian women‚Äôs environmental engagement predates the formal label of ecofeminism; traditional roles in water management, agriculture and forest-based livelihoods made women custodians of ecological knowledge.
Ecofeminism in India is not only theoretical, but it is lived, practised, and continually negotiated in movements where gender, ecology, culture and community intersect. This Earth Day, take a look at some major women-led environmental movements.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read the full article by Ekta Singh, a PhD Scholar in the Discipline of Gender Studies at Banaras Hindu University. | Views expressed by the author are their own.

posts.caption:
We often witness mothers-in-law bestowing gold jewellery upon their daughters-in-law during weddings, often remarking how the piece is pushtaini (ancestral), once passed down to them by their own mothers-in-law. This tradition of passing down ornaments goes far beyond mere ritual. These heirlooms were originally given with love and care, intended to ensure the financial security of the women in the family. Even decades later, gold and property retain substantial value, safeguarding future generations against financial instability. Generations ago, gold wasn't just ornamental-they were a woman's rainy-day fund, a lifeline in times of uncertainty.
Unlike today, passing down property or gold wasn't a transaction or tool of control-it was a safeguard. According to the Arthashastra, a woman's ownership of Stridhana was legally recognised. Women had full rights to own, manage, and even dispose of their Stridhana, including the freedom to pass it on to their daughters or other relatives. In a society where men were traditionally the primary earners and women managed homes, Stridhana gave women a sense of financial agency and independence.
Before SIPs and stocks, our grandmothers saved in the form of gold. Today, in an age of credit cards, EMIs, and instant gratification, this wisdom remains quietly revolutionary. The most powerful legacy we inherit from the women before us isn't just the gold in our lockers-it's the foresight to prepare for life's uncertainties with grit and unwavering self-worth.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read the full article by @iamkhushidwivedi | Views expressed by the author are their own.
