posts.caption:
A note before you comment: STUDIO gives you access to our free 7 day trial of The Studio of Stylist Elixir, our membership community at ¬£20 a month. We also share free guides and resources on Instagram and a free Substack every Wednesday. üíö
Fashion styling has a rate. It always has. The problem is that this industry has never been transparent about what that rate is, and for decades stylists have absorbed the consequences of that silence.
The work starts before the shoot. The expertise takes years to build. The pressure is carried quietly so nobody else has to feel it. And when that work generates commercial value for a brand, a client, or a campaign, it is not a favour. It is a job. And jobs have rates.
We polled over 1,200 stylists across every sector of the industry because we believe that access to this information should not be a privilege. It should be the starting point.
If you are a stylist who has ever quoted blind, undercharged because you were not sure, or struggled to explain your worth to a client, this is for you.
If you are a client, a brand, or someone who has ever wondered what a fashion stylist actually charges and why, this is for you too.
Comment STUDIO and we will send you access to the full rate breakdown inside The Studio of Stylist Elixir. üíö‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
‚û°Ô∏è‚û°Ô∏è If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.

posts.caption: If something needs such in-depth analysis to be understood than the assignment was a failure! It‚Äôs not a dissertation on the politics of 17th century France. It‚Äôs CLOTHING! It‚Äôs a fashion show and since fashion shows have been able to be seen by millions and consumed in photos and video for decades, those who are not in the loop to see the clothes for real are only left with what we see and to dare to say that just because a design has ‚ÄúReferences‚Äù or inner history negates it from being judged as what it simply LOOKS LIKE, is insultingly arrogant, something that takes 4,000 hours of the most intricate and elaborate technique‚Ä¶ CAN STILL LOOK HORRID! All that time does not acquit it of its ugliness or stupidity of design! When all that history and back story sgd references WORK IN CONJUNCTION with the clothes to crest something magical (Blazy/Chanel) then the framework of all that in depth research and application of history is breathtaking, when it‚Äôs like JWA/Dior, it just comes across as over focus on ideas that then translate into nothing more than dissertations on a thought than the true purpose of what is happening‚Ä¶ MAKING BEAUTIFUL CLOTHES! And for you to suggest that unless you‚Äôre erudite and informed of history that you shouldn‚Äôt comment is the height of condescension! Anyone can see! And no matter how layered the meaning, if it looks like all that time taken to produce garbage still looks like GARBAGE, it changes not its beauty because you have the knowing of the thought processes and HISTOIRE D‚ÄôMAISON behind you! It‚Äôs pretentious and asinine

posts.caption:
There is a misconception about what a fashion stylist actually does.
Most people think it is picking the outfit. Choosing the dress. Showing up on shoot day. That is the visible part. It is also five percent of the job.
The actual job is the work nobody sees. The brief. The research. The references. The pull. The fittings. The alterations. The packing. The travel. The returns. The shipping. The invoices. The follow ups. The kit. The team. The protection of the work itself.
A fashion stylist is a project manager, a researcher, a logistics co-ordinator, a relationship manager, and a creative director, who happens to know what looks good on a body.
The day rate is for all of it.
And then there are the things a stylist should not be doing. Running the career like a hobby. Working for free. Picking outfits over text. Working for credit. Absorbing the costs. None of these are the job. All of them get asked for. All of them get politely turned down.
With love, Em x
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on April 25, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine and text that says 'What a Fashion Stylist Actually Does. And What They Do Not. Stylist Elixi'.

posts.caption:
There is a scarcity around the rate of a fashion stylist. And there are a number of reasons for it.
We love gatekeeping. We do not want people knowing what we charge because we charge different clients different amounts. The industry is saturated with stylists undercutting. The top dogs who run the industry do not talk, will not share rates, and are generally stuck in their old ways. Their world is not affected, so they do nothing to help.
Fair enough.
But what about everyone else? What about the stylists in the middle? The ones who do not have the top celebrity clients. The ones who do not live in a fashion city. The up and coming ones who do not know what to charge. The ones who have been in it ten years and are still guessing.
That is who this is for‚ùóÔ∏è
Every stylist is different. Every client is different. Every job is different. Different rates in different sectors of styling. Different rates in different countries. Some countries have active unions helping stylists. Some do not. Some parts of the styling world are rate regulated. Some are not.
So no. This is not telling you what to charge.
This is how to work it out.
The Rate Build. Five parts. Run every time a new enquiry comes in.
Scope. Tier. Load. Extras. Minimum.
The framework is in the carousel. The data that goes with it is inside The Studio of Stylist Elixir, a paid membership community for fashion stylists. Rate guides built from data across every sector. Pitch frameworks. Portfolio teardowns. Sourcing systems. Contract templates. Community calls. Everything you need to price, pitch, protect, and grow a styling career.
Comment RATES for a free trial link.
With love, Em x
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on April 24, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine, dress and text that says 'Fashion Stylists: How to Calculate Your Rate Stylist StylistElixir Elixir'.

posts.caption:
Undercharging does not just cost a stylist money.
It costs time. Unpaid prep days. Expenses absorbed. Hours working beyond the brief that never make it onto the invoice. It costs the right clients. The ones paying properly are not going to book a stylist whose rates suggest otherwise. It costs confidence. Every rate dropped takes a small part of the language to defend the next one. It costs relationships. The partner who watches the stylist work late again. The friends who stop getting replies. The family asking when this is going to start paying.
And it costs the industry. A generation of talented stylists leaving because the numbers never added up. Every stylist who walks away is a point of view the industry never got to keep.
The fee is not the only cost. It never has been.
The Studio of Stylist Elixir is the membership platform for fashion stylists who are done absorbing what the industry will not pay for. Rate guides by sector. Editorial, commercial, celebrity. Scripts for when a client pushes back. Green lights for when to raise. Red flags for the clients to walk away from. And the community of stylists inside finally talking to each other about money, which is the part that actually changes things.
Built from what over 1,200 stylists told us they charge.
Seven days free. Twenty pounds a month after that.
Comment STUDIO for a free trial link

posts.caption:
Priyanka Shah (@pri_ism) is a creative director, stylist, and artist based in Mumbai. Trained as a communication designer, her practice has evolved from graphic design into a richly tactile, multidisciplinary language that sits at the intersection of art, design, craft and narrative building. After practising communication design for seven years, Shah grew creatively restless and quit her job to start a self-initiated project involving foraging botanicals to create plant-based sculptures. ‚ÄúIt was instinctive and exploratory. Less about outcome, more about process,‚Äù she notes.
It was this impulse toward the tactile that culminated in the founding of Studio Lalala, a practice that is rooted in immersive storytelling. The studio‚Äôs body of work now spans installations, creating spatial environments, styling, and designing sculptural objects. At its core, the practice is less about the medium and more about the narrative and approach. ‚ÄúAt its core, my work is about storytelling through material. Whether we are creating a window display, styling a shoot, designing an installation, or building absculptural object, the intention is the same: to create an experience that evokes emotion and curiosity.‚Äù
Growing up, creativity wasn‚Äôt just a hobby for her, but a part of her life, with her father taking her to work and showing her screen printing, offset printing, and various other industrial processes. ‚ÄúMy parents always encouraged curiosity and experimentation. Looking back, I realise that my practice is simply an extension of that childhood, of being fascinated by materials, processes, and the magic of making.‚Äù This same curiosity now
informs everything she does, whether in installations, object-making, or fantastical environments; the core of the practice is informed by research, process, and deep
exploration. ‚ÄúI really, really enjoy diving into new  materials, learning from artisans, and pushing ourselves into unfamiliar territories. Learning as we go is a fundamental part of the studio‚Äôs DNA.‚Äù
üì∏: Priyanka Shah (@pri_ism)

posts.caption: Yup. I am all for sustained efforts to integrate NE in both physical connectivity and spiritual connectivity. But I would also implore you to reduce the mainland-peripehery dissonance within. And a common person sitting anywhere in India has no ill will either. For centuries, physical disconnect has resulted in this. And decades of mishandling by the govt has done no favor. But, this is changing rapidly with a digital connection, sustained focus of current GOI to highlight NE among the "mainland" masses and transport. Now, here's the kicker... The phenomenon pointed out with a negative lens, is proof that assimilation and integration is increasing. For example, You need to understand that an average Person isn't thinking about the "significance" of momos and is willing to shell out 10x material cost for history. That's the stuff of luxury and would be the real action of "aesthetic-isation" of momos. When NERs product sits behind the elite doors. And bringing the examples of banarasi saree, petha, kachori is significant because they show us difference between aesthetic isation and acceptance. Today, banaras and mumbai may feel very close to you or jaipur and Gujrat or even just two cities in a state, that wasn't always the case. When Agra ka petha started being sold in the street corners of Bikaner... Merchants had to navigate terrain, bandits, distance, fatigue.. everything to traverse between two. It was no easier than Mumbai ‚Üí NER in current times. That's what you can see as the initial stage of integration... When a place became synonymous with your favorite sweet. Next came familiarity, proliferation of skills etc. leading to "mainland". Otherwise a maithli speaking person is as distant to a marwadi speaking person. In this scenario, would you criticise the merchant, the consumer, the seller? No. No one's at fault. One thing we have the luxury of doing though is to tell people about the significance.. instead of criticising their ignorance. Just imagine, if in every slide.. there was a tidbit mentioned about the significance of the product mentioned. Wouldn't that do more towards recognition than this post does? Continued...

Ozempic in India moved from a niche indulgence to something far more everyday in a very short span. Trainers and chemists are part of the distribution, and women are using it at significantly higher rates than men. The benefits are real ‚Äî improved metabolic health, support for PCOS ‚Äî but the context has shifted.
What was once used as therapy is increasingly being approached as a shortcut, and the risks are beginning to show. It‚Äôs the wellness solution everyone is talking about. The question is what that access is actually building towards.
Read more at the link in bio.
By Rituparna Som ( @ritusom )

posts.caption:
Heartbreak doesn‚Äôt stay internal for long. It tends to show up in ways that are easy to spot. The haircut. The tattoo. The shift that feels small but rarely is.
What reads as impulse is often something else. A way of taking control when very little feels steady, or putting distance between who someone was and who they are trying to become.
When loss disrupts routine and identity, changing something visible offers a clear, immediate response. It doesn‚Äôt resolve what‚Äôs underneath, but it does mark that something has already changed.
Read more on the link in bio
By Ria Bhatia ( @riabbhatia )
#griefcoping #bodyimage #wellnessculture #traumaandidentity #postbreakupglowup

posts.caption:
No one orders the same thing at the table.
Groups arrive with mixed preferences, generations, dietary needs and uneven familiarity with cuisines. One menu holds all of it. That‚Äôs where Pan Asian restaurants come in. A single menu spanning multiple Asian cuisines.
Chinese cuisine anchors that experience. It has been around the longest and has adapted to Indian tastes, making it easier to introduce Japanese, Korean, Thai or Vietnamese dishes into the same meal.
Even as single-cuisine restaurants grow, Pan Asian continues to work because of how India eats, social, shared and built around variety.
This isn‚Äôt new. Head to the link in bio to see why Pan Asian restaurants continue to dominate and what they reveal about eating out in India.
By Karishma Kuenzang ( @kkuenzang )
#PanAsian #DiningTrends #FoodCulture #IndianDining #RestaurantTrends

posts.caption:
ADHD in women often reads as competence: multitasking, perfectionism, emotional labour. Underneath, it‚Äôs chronic overwhelm, time blindness, and exhaustion.
Long  framed as a male disorder, ADHD symptoms in women are overlooked, or misread  as stress. The result is years of self-blame before recognition. Masking, hormonal shifts, and the cost of care in India continue to delay diagnosis and limit access to treatment.
The system isn‚Äôt missing ADHD in women. It‚Äôs been trained not to see it.
Read more at the link in bio
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)
#ADHD #MentalHealthAwareness #WomensHealth #Neurodiversity #Wellness

posts.caption:
Shalom Harlow was the 90s ‚Äúanti-supermodel,‚Äù and that‚Äôs exactly why she defined the era. Discovered at a The Cure concert in 1990, she brought a completely different energy to the runway: less polished perfection, more instinctive, slightly offbeat cool. Designers like Versace and McQueen literally built moments around her presence.
She made high fashion feel effortless but never boring. Elegant, but with an edge. The kind of model who could carry a show without looking like she was trying to.
By 1995, she took home the industry‚Äôs first Model of the Year award, cementing what everyone already knew: she‚Äôs that girl.
#ShalomHarlow #90sFashion #SupermodelEra #FashionHistory #TheCitizensPoste

Built around the SS26 Studio collection, the space leans into fluid tailoring, elevated basics, and that very specific quiet luxury energy the brand is clearly doubling down on.
But the real draw is the environment. Vintage furniture sourced from @galeriepatrickseguin and @jacqueslacoste, a curated literary selection from @librairieyvonlambert, coffee from @cafenuances, and a lineup of talks around design and curation.

posts.caption:
If you‚Äôre looking for a designer who treats fashion like theory, start with Hussein Chalayan. Often called fashion‚Äôs ‚Äúmad professor,‚Äù Chalayan has never been interested in clothes as just clothes. His work sits somewhere between philosophy, science, and performance art‚Äîusing garments to explore identity, migration, technology, and time itself.
He first broke through in 1993 with his graduate collection The Tangent Flows, where he buried silk dresses in a garden for three months to study decay, then sent them down the runway as living artifacts.
What sets Chalayan apart is that the technology in his work is never just for spectacle. Whether it‚Äôs transforming garments or conceptual staging, everything is in service of a deeper narrative about where we‚Äôve been and where we‚Äôre going.
#HusseinChalayan #FashionHistory #ConceptualFashion #DesignThinking #TheCitizensPoste

posts.caption: Warning to anyone wanting to buy with Vestiaire‚Ä¶. Don‚Äôt do it. Bought a LV purse but instantly questioned authenticity (literally as soon as I purchased it) went straight on chat about this. They kindly offered to verify it. The purse came to me and it definitely appeared fake to me‚Ä¶. No heat stamp inside, no serial number, very thin cheap dust bag. Hardware not typical of LV. Sent it off for verification. Waited a few days and today I get a notification to say it‚Äôs been verified and is authentic‚Ä¶. ü´¢ I do not believe it for a second. Go on to chat to discuss, they say it‚Äôs got a serial number (seller also told me no serial number as well as when I looked myself) so I asked where the serial number is and could I have a picture‚Ä¶ bearing in mind now they have it they are refusing to refund me and they are listing it to be resold. They‚Äôve said they can‚Äôt send me a picture and can‚Äôt provide me where it is. Which all makes no sense to me whatsoever. I‚Äôve reminded them that me asking for that serial number is me asking for that of my property. I don‚Äôt believe it‚Äôs real and therefore don‚Äôt wish to keep it but it is mine. Asked to speak to a manager and denied. I mean I may get a refund if it sells in 2yrs? What if it doesn‚Äôt sell at all? I imagine that I won‚Äôt see that listing and really they will do anything with it because they have it in their possession even though it‚Äôs mine?? Absolutely vile company. I am a small buyer I‚Äôve not spent thousands. I know me not purchasing will make no difference really but I did want to warn others. I will never use them again, I‚Äôm going to make a claim on PayPal and leave a trust pilot review‚Ä¶. It‚Äôs a good job I guess that it wasn‚Äôt something worth hundreds or thousands. Very very bad company.

posts.caption:
Manoj Bajpayee (@bajpayee.manoj) is ridiculously‚ÄØyouthful-looking‚ÄØfor a 50-something.‚ÄØHe‚Äôs‚ÄØtrim, his face glows,‚ÄØhe‚Äôs‚ÄØlight on his feet. His body of work is already impressive‚ÄØand curated, and his‚ÄØphysical self suggests‚ÄØthe same level of‚ÄØcare and attention.‚ÄØ‚ÄúYou sacrifice a lot and don‚Äôt compromise with your daily routine,‚Äù says Bajpayee. It‚Äôs‚ÄØbeen 17 years since he started skipping dinners, fitness advice that has‚ÄØnow become‚ÄØa habit.‚ÄØHis work follows the same rigour.
From the beginning, there‚Äôs been a palpable hunger in Bajpayee‚Äôs progression‚Äîan unwavering journey towards excellence that has been present ever since he became the centrepiece of Ram Gopal Varma‚Äôs classic crime drama ‚ÄòSatya‚Äô as the flamboyant gangster Bhiku Mhatre in 1998. Over the years, the actor became the name to go to for films and shows that demanded an assiduous unpacking of characters with his ability to not just inhabit a part but also make its impact linger beyond the end credits. Today, he‚Äôs at the top of his game, occupying a position that once was the late Irrfan‚Äôs, straddling mainstream, indie, and middle-of-the-road projects.
Nila Iyer (@nilaiyer) sits down with Bajpayee to discuss films, family, and his 30-year-long career.

posts.caption:
Manoj Bajpayee‚Äôs (@bajpayee.manoj) filmography is a ‚Äúmixed bag‚Äù, he points out‚Äîby design. His upcoming projects include ‚ÄòGovernor‚Äô directed by Chinmay Mandlekar, and a reunion with Ram Gopal Varma for the horror comedy ‚ÄòPolice Station Mein Bhoot‚Äô. Neeraj Pandey‚Äôs crime thriller for Netflix is also in the pipeline, as is American director Ben Rekhi‚Äôs upcoming adaptation of Arvind Adiga‚Äôs novel ‚ÄòLast Man in Tower‚Äô.
Right now, Bajpayee is at that sweet spot in his career where he can take the long view. On screen, he has played losers and heroes, but IRL he‚Äôs able to balance celebrity with craft and Bollywood‚Äôs ruthless demands with his individual ambitions. For him, mainstream cinema is a strategic choice. ‚ÄúThe Hindi film industry is very market-oriented, so to keep one‚Äôs value alive one has to balance it with things on which I don‚Äôt compromise,‚Äù Bajpayee explains.
Read Nila Iyer‚Äôs (@nilaiyer) full interview with the Bollywood actor at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Last year, designer and Madraswear founder Purushu Arie accused Chennai-based designer Vivek Karunakaran of copying his work‚Äîhis innovations with tailored lungis, ùêØùêûùê≠ùê≠ùê¢ùê¨ and a distinctly Tamil design language. Karunakaran maintained that his work was independently developed.
At ùëªùëΩùë∂ùë≠, we invited both to present their views‚Äîon design and copyright. The counterpoint, however, never ran. Because Arie‚Äôs argument moved beyond design authorship into caste, class, and privilege. We did not want not adjudicate the caste versus privilege terrain within a design plagiarism debate. I saw them as relevant but separate issues. Arie withdrew his article. We respected that decision.
Yet, part of what he wrote stayed with me. ‚ÄúMy design authorship cannot be detached from its politics of assertion and resistance. Without that context, it is reduced to surface ornamentation‚Äîlike temple colour schemes on Madras checks.‚Äù
Temple colours on Madras checks was a stinging metaphor. That what is worn on the street, shaped by stigma and social geography, is acceptable only after validation. Arie said he had been mocked for his work until validated by the press. Innovation from the margins he believes is often dismissed.
The argument knocks on a door that the fashion industry rarely opens.
April is Dalit History Month.
Yet, not much on caste has surfaced in Indian fashion media‚Äînot this month, not across the year. Indian fashion speaks fluently about craft. It debates cultural appropriation, now increasingly so, given the growing disregard to Indian-origin work and skills by Western brands. Textile-craft exhibitions foreground artisan visibility. But when it comes to caste‚Äîwithin which much of the labour exists‚Äîthe conversation falls silent.
As Suraj Yengde writes in ùë™ùíÇùíîùíïùíÜ ùë¥ùíÇùíïùíïùíÜùíìùíî, caste is not a system waiting to disappear; it is graded hierarchy that mutates, and survives within modern institutions.
Fashion is no exception. It wears hierarchy on its sleeve. The lead image from Chamar Studio applauds its founder Sudheer Rajbhar for turning a slur into a brand. Read the rest on the link in bio.
#dalithistorymonth #casteinfashion #politicsofdesign
üñãÔ∏è @shefatwork

posts.caption:
From the Ralph Lauren bandhani-print skirt and ùíãùíâùíñùíéùíåùíÇ earrings, to Dior‚Äôs recent take on the ùíÉùíÇùíèùíÖùíâùíàùíÇùíçùíÇ and Prada‚Äôs Kolhapuri-inspired leather footwear‚Äîglobal fashion‚Äôs fascination with repackaging Indian crafts and textiles and giving them a six-figure price tag is peaking in the debates on cultural appropriation. As Ritu Sethi, founder-trustee of the Craft Revival Trust, points out: ‚ÄúColonised nations have long been emporiums of patterns, designs and products that those in power have been picking and choosing from for many centuries. That is why the ùíÇùíéùíÉùíä became the paisley, and you will see the ubiquitous ‚ÄòIndian prints‚Äô or ‚ÄòAfrican design‚Äô in books.‚Äù
But in a market where motifs, techniques and narratives are copied and mass-produced‚Äîboth by the domestic unorganised sector and international players‚Äî Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) is what can separate authenticity from cultural extraction. Safir Anand, senior partner and head (Trademarks, Contractual and Commercial IP) at Delhi-based law firm Anand & Anand believes that the protection of Indian legacy often fails not because tools are unavailable, but because they are misunderstood.
This article, that first appeared in ùëªùíâùíÜ ùëΩùíêùíäùíÑùíÜ ùíêùíá ùë≠ùíÇùíîùíâùíäùíêùíè'ùíî luxury white paper ùëªùíâùíÜ ùë®ùíïùíçùíÇùíî ùíêùíá ùë®ùíáùíáùíçùíñùíÜùíèùíÑùíÜ 2026 on The Rise of Indian Luxury, examines how copyrights can safeguard creative expression and why filing for protection and taking legal recourse is no longer optional for Indian brands. Link in bio.

posts.caption:
Hi, I‚Äôm Veronica üëãüèº a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio ‚Äî each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You‚Äôll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
‚Ä¢ Shirring Calculator ‚Äì works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
‚Ä¢ GSM Calculator ‚Äì estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com

posts.caption:
I rewatched Anastasia and then spent three hours finding out everything I didn‚Äôt know about it as a kid. Fair warning: this film is not historically accurate, and by the end of these slides you‚Äôll understand exactly why that matters.
Also how did we KNOW the blue dress was velvet without knowing it was velvet?
But first, Meg Ryan had to be convinced to take the role, Liz Callaway sang every song you love, and Jim Cummings voiced two of the most iconic villain songs of the 90s without getting nearly enough credit for either.
The further I went, the more complicated it got. The film is built on a rumour that was already disproven before it came out. It gave the revolution a face so the Romanovs didn‚Äôt have to have one. And Anastasia was canonised as a saint in 2000, a detail Western nostalgia tends to skip past entirely.
I still love this film. I‚Äôm just sitting with it differently now. What‚Äôs your favourite part? üôä

posts.caption:
We surveyed hundreds of Vogue and GQ readers about their wants from #AI in fashion. The key takeaway? Consumers are cautious, and the execution of early applications of AI in fashion is what‚Äôs holding them back right now. Those who have adopted its use for shopping are driven primarily by convenience and efficiency, but many are constrained by concerns around trust and authenticity. Tap the link in bio, where tech editor @AmyEOBrien and senior reporter @MalihaShoaib_ further unpack the survey‚Äôs findings ‚Äî as part of our series the ‚ÄòFuture of AI‚Äô.

posts.caption:
What happens when you put Meryl Streep and Anna Wintour‚Äîthe two Mirandas, as it were‚Äîin a room together? A wide-ranging conversation about fashion, family, friendship, and yes, the forthcoming sequel to ‚ÄúThe Devil Wears Prada.‚Äù
‚ÄúIn terms of Miranda, and coming back to that character 20 years later, I did think honestly about Anna, and tried to imagine what it was like to carry her responsibility and to be as interested in the world and curious as she must have to be,‚Äù Streep tells Greta Gerwig in Vogue‚Äôs May 2026 cover story. And what did Wintour do when she heard the rumours that ‚ÄúThe Devil Wears Prada 2‚Äù was happening? She called Streep, of course. ‚ÄúI knew she would tell me if it was going to be all right,‚Äù Wintour says. ‚ÄúI trusted her implicitly.‚Äù
Read the full story at the link in bio.

posts.caption: At Dries Van Noten‚Äôs Fondazione debut presentation, opening today in Venice, an onslaught of intoxicating meraviglie awaits. Within the lavish embrace of Palazzo Pisani Moretta, whose stuccoed ceilings and ornate rooms are a manifesto of 18th century decorative excess, beauty isn‚Äôt simply in residence, it proliferates. Fashion, craft, and art converse fluently, each amplifying the other in a prismatic Wunderkammer, shaped by Van Noten‚Äôs almost alchemical curatorial instinct. The designer sat down with @tiziana_cardini to discuss the historic palazzo that houses the presentation, why fashion is merely a punctuation mark in this expansive show, and, naturally, the power of beauty.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on April 25, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'VOGUE RUNWAY RUN INSIDE THE INAUGURAL PRESENTATION AT DRIES VAN NOTEN'S NEW FONDAZIONE IN VENICE'.

posts.caption: This week is the fifth anniversary of Alber Elbaz‚Äôs passing. The designer brought a certain joie de vivre, wit, intuition, empathy, and spontaneous flair to fashion. The kind of thinking he applied to how women are living. The discussion behind it. How he just wanted to make complicated things easy and delightful. Half a decade is a long time, but the relevance of his approach is still on point for now. His clothes are still alive in closets, being worn as favorite fail-safes for evening. They‚Äôre in museums and archives, and being purloined by daughters and granddaughters. At the link in bio, @SarahMower_ remembers Alber Elbaz, the beloved designer of Lanvin and AZ Factory, five years after his death.

posts.caption: There are fashion obsessives, and then there‚Äôs @jeandenisfranoux. A longtime fashion professor at Studio Ber√ßot and a designer himself from 1994 to 2001, Franoux began collecting perfume bottles as a child before getting hooked on clothes. Today, at 56, Franoux is sitting on an archive of 25,000 pieces and counting and from tonight through Saturday, an invitation-only event at a private space in the Upper Marais will offer a first look at the collective achievement, titled Regarderobes (the name is a portmanteau of the word ‚Äúlook‚Äù and ‚Äúwardrobes‚Äù). Days before the opening, he welcomed Vogue to his Paris apartment/storeroom/makeshift photo studio, where hundreds of finds line racks while others, piled in boxes from floor to ceiling, await their close-up. Read more about what to expect at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on April 23, 2026. May be an image of overcoat, display case, mannequin and text.

posts.caption:
@nathanhartono's creative appetite knows no bounds. Whilst some might bask in overnight success, the hometown hero has built his reputation through the years‚Äîcontinuing to explore the multitudinal facets of his world by dabbling in various passions, from music to theatre. "I have an insatiable curiosity towards all these different sides, and am still learning more and more every day," our #VogueMan cover star quips.
For our #VogueMan 'Pursuit' issue, the singer-songwriter and actor is captured in numerous unexpected spots dotted around Singapore‚Äîeach frame teeming with the refreshingly zany mood that seems to colour Hartono's life.
In the link in bio, Hartono delves into his creative worlds, new projects he is working on, and how community sits at the centre of the Singapore arts scene. Preorder a copy of our #VogueMan 'Pursuit' issue, out on newsstands on 15 April.

"Each discipline fulfils a different creative side of me. Acting allows me to tell stories, music feels very personal, and modelling connects me with fashion and visual expression," shares #RebeccaArmstrong, who joins us on our April 'Retrofuture' issue as one of two cover stars.
In the link in bio, we delve into the new creative challenges she's taking on in 2026, and what she's been learning about herself along the way.

posts.caption:
@caileespaeny is in between worlds. Having played characters that span across time and space, the Golden Globe-nominated actress has a fascinating plurality about her that feels both familiar and fresh.
Cast in a nostalgic mood that calls to mind the filmography of #DavidLynch and #StanleyKubrick, the actress transforms into a defiant, enigmatic persona as our 'Retrofuture' cover star, lensed by the legendary Canadian photographer @normanjeanroy. "For the shoot, we were tapping into a bit of a David Lynch world. It was so fun to lock into this set and character with the team on this shoot," Spaeny muses.
As one of two cover stars for April's 'Retrofuture' issue, Spaeny lets us into her universe‚Äîfrom her determined portrayal of Rain Carradine in 'Alien: Romulus' to the tender portrait she paints in 'Priscilla' and her newest work for @netflix's limited series 'Beef'. The full cover story, now in the link in bio.

In the word ‚ÄúShikargah‚Äù , ‚ÄúShikar‚Äù means hunting and ‚ÄúGah‚Äù means place.
The Shikargah by Divani is representative of a limited edition couture series reinterpreted with finest ‚Äú zardozi | ý§úý§ºý§∞ý§¶ý•ãý§úý§ºý•Ä | ÿ≤ÿ±ÿØŸàÿ≤€å " and the old school techniques of ‚Äútukdi ‚Äù patching using hand-dyed and handwoven tissue silk. From depicting the typical lion and deer hunt to british influences in designs can be notably seen as a part of the hunting scenes.
Featured here is our limited edition ‚ÄúShikargah‚Äù saree, adorned with handcrafted animal and floral motifs on pure muga silk. The look is completed with an ornate jacket from the same series, featuring rich flora and fauna motifs rendered as an all-over pattern with archival techniques. posts.caption:
Rooted in the revival of royal costumes and heirloom traditions, this Farshi Garara traces back to the courts of Awadh and Hyderabad, known for its floor-length, sweeping silhouette.
Featured here is Divani‚Äôs classic Farshi Garara in a deep crimson tones, detailed with "lucknawi tukdi ka kaam" and crafted with pure copper wire zardozi craftsmanship. It is paired with a signature kurti, adorned with handcrafted peacock detailings. For the purposes of styling, the ensemble is completed with a "Hyderabadi khada dupatta", an heirloom drape from the royal wardrobes.
Crafted in pure silk tissue, this piece reflects the revival of handloom textiles through our Benarasi looms, bringing back the essence of royal dressings.

posts.caption:
The Great Gatsby. Promising Young Woman. Maestro. There‚Äôs no shortage of career highlights for Carey Mulligan. Our April cover star has never shied away from portraying complicated women with rich inner lives, and now, she‚Äôs stepping into her messiest role yet. ‚ÄúI was like, ‚ÄòOh, that‚Äôs insane and feral. And I loved it,‚Äô‚Äù Mulligan recalls of reading one particularly unhinged scene in season 2 of Beef, the Emmy-winning dark comedy-drama miniseries in which her character‚Äôs marriage devolves into resentment, blackmail, and regret. With the sophomore season hitting Netflix this month and Greta Gerwig‚Äôs highly anticipated Narnia adaptation coming later this year, the actress has her hands full with one buzzy project after another. From Mulligan‚Äôs real marriage advice to her dream projects, go deeper into our April cover story at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
I know ‚Äúchic‚Äù gets thrown around a lot, but this dress genuinely defines it. #carolynbessettekennedy 1996 wedding gown a bias-cut silk slip with a soft cowl neckline stood in sharp contrast to the era‚Äôs oversized, heavily embellished bridal styles, instantly reshaping modern bridal fashion.
Created by her close friend Narciso Rodriguez, whom she met during her time at Calvin Klein, the design drew global attention and propelled his career, highlighting his refined tailoring and quiet sensuality.
Though documentation is scarce, Love Story costume designer Rudy Mance painstakingly recreated the gown, sourcing original fabrics and collaborating with the same makers behind the veil and gloves. Through multiple fittings, the dress was perfected to capture the precise bias cut and fluid elegance that made the original unforgettable.

posts.caption:
Inside #louisvuitton latest spectacle in London, a grand Mayfair townhouse has been transformed into a lavish, multi-floor ‚Äúhotel‚Äù experience celebrating 130 years of its iconic monogram.
Though not an actual place to stay, the pop-up blends fashion, hospitality, and exhibition immersing visitors in themed rooms inspired by signature bags like the Speedy, Keepall, and Neverfull.
Guests ‚Äúcheck in‚Äù through the Keepall Lobby before exploring spaces like Caf√© Alma for Parisian-style dining and Bar No√©, an intimate champagne lounge.
Each floor acts as a storytelling canvas, turning luxury travel heritage into a theatrical, Instagram-ready experience rather than a traditional retail store.
Open for a limited time in Berkeley Square, it‚Äôs less about accommodation and more about positioning Louis Vuitton as a lifestyle universe where fashion, culture, and fantasy collide.
#louisvuitton #lv #womenculture
